context:
Section 7 (Future Research Directions), page 31, first bullet point.

A related question is: how well can one approximate an n-dimensional symmetric convex body with a finite intersection of m (co-centered) ellipsoids, with m growing potentially with n? Since we have shown that a GE-d, with d = 2m−3, can exactly represent an intersection of m co-centered ellipsoids (see Corollary 5.6 and Theorem 5.1), progress on this question can potentially improve our approximation factor in Theorem 5.7.
title: Approximation quality of convex bodies by intersections of co-centered ellipsoids
background_latex:
Let \(C\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) be a symmetric convex body, i.e., a compact convex set with nonempty interior such that \(x\in C\Rightarrow -x\in C\).

An (origin-centered) ellipsoid is a set \(E(P)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: x^\top P x\le 1\}\) for some positive definite matrix \(P\in \mathbb{S}^n_{++}\). An \emph{intersection of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids} is a set of the form
\[K=\bigcap_{i=1}^m E(P_i),\qquad P_i\in \mathbb{S}^n_{++}.\]
Such sets are symmetric convex bodies.

For two symmetric convex bodies \(K,C\subset\mathbb{R}^n\), a standard multiplicative approximation notion is: \(K\subseteq C\subseteq \alpha K\) for some \(\alpha\ge 1\), where \(\alpha K:=\{\alpha x: x\in K\}\).

A related family introduced in the paper is that of generalized ellipsoids of degree \(d\) (GE-\(d\)), which can exactly represent intersections of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids with \(d\le 2m-3\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine, as a function of \(n\) and \(m\), how well an \(n\)-dimensional symmetric convex body \(C\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) can be approximated by an intersection of \(m\) co-centered ellipsoids.

Concretely, characterize (or bound) the smallest \(\alpha\ge 1\) such that for every symmetric convex body \(C\subset\mathbb{R}^n\) there exist \(P_1,\dots,P_m\in\mathbb{S}^n_{++}\) with
\[\bigcap_{i=1}^m \{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: x^\top P_i x\le 1\}\subseteq C \subseteq \alpha\, \bigcap_{i=1}^m \{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: x^\top P_i x\le 1\}.\]
(Equivalently, give the best achievable approximation factor and its dependence on \(n\) and \(m\), allowing \(m\) to grow with \(n\).)

open_problem_quote: A related question is: how well can one approximate an n-dimensional symmetric convex body with a finite intersection of m (co-centered) ellipsoids, with m growing potentially with n? Since we have shown that a GE-d, with d = 2m−3, can exactly represent an intersection of m co-centered ellipsoids (see Corollary 5.6 and Theorem 5.1), progress on this question can potentially improve our approximation factor in Theorem 5.7.
subject_classification: convex geometry and semidefinite-representable convex sets
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is asymptotic/worst-case over all symmetric convex bodies in \(\mathbb{R}^n\), so finite-dimensional instance search is unlikely to yield definitive bounds except for very small \(n\) and \(m\). Even then, representing the space of all convex bodies (or hard families) is nontrivial.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no clear automatically evaluable fitness signal for candidate theorems or bounds, because verifying optimality of approximation factors requires worst-case analysis over an infinite class of convex bodies. While one could numerically test candidate constructions on sampled bodies, this would not certify theoretical guarantees.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
